name: Watanabe no Tsuna
description: Watanabe no Tsuna, also known as Tsuna-Genji, was a prominent samurai and retainer of Minamoto no Yorimitsu (Raiko), one of the most famous warrior figures in Japanese folklore. He was a member of the elite group known as the Shitennō, the Four Heavenly Kings, who served Yorimitsu. Tsuna was renowned for his incredible strength, bravery, and loyalty, and he is the subject of numerous tales and legends, often involving encounters with demons and supernatural creatures. His most famous exploit involves severing the arm of the demon Ibaraki-dōji at the Rashōmon gate in Kyoto. This act cemented his reputation as a fearless warrior and a protector of the people. Tsuna's stories are often used to exemplify the virtues of a true samurai, including courage, honor, and unwavering dedication to his lord. He is remembered as a powerful figure who stood against the forces of evil and helped to maintain order in a turbulent era. Beyond his martial prowess, Tsuna's tales often explore themes of deception, transformation, and the blurred lines between the human and supernatural realms. His legacy continues to inspire and captivate audiences, ensuring his place as a legendary hero in Japanese folklore. His story is a testament to the enduring power of human courage in the face of overwhelming odds, and he remains a symbol of strength and resilience.
subtitle: Demon Slayer and Loyal Retainer
achievements: Serving as one of the Shitennō
Displaying unwavering loyalty to Minamoto no Yorimitsu
Becoming a symbol of samurai bravery
companions: Other members of the Shitennō (e.g., Sakata no Kintoki, Urabe no Suetake)

keyMyths.title: Severing Ibaraki-dōji's Arm
keyMyths.description: Watanabe no Tsuna encountered the demon Ibaraki-dōji at the Rashōmon gate and, in a fierce battle, severed its arm. The demon fled, leaving its arm behind as a trophy of Tsuna's victory and a testament to his strength.
keyMyths.source: Various folklore collections and theatrical performances

keyMyths.title: The Encounter at Modoribashi Bridge
keyMyths.description: A beautiful woman asked Tsuna for a ride across Modoribashi Bridge. Mid-crossing, she transformed into a demon, but Tsuna, unfazed, forced her to release him and fled. This showcases his ability to withstand supernatural trickery.
keyMyths.source: Various folklore collections and theatrical performances

keyMyths.title: Tsuna's Dream and the Arm
keyMyths.description: After severing Ibaraki-dōji's arm, Tsuna kept it locked away. His aunt visited and requested to see it, only to transform into the demon and steal it back, showing the demon's cunning and Tsuna's momentary lapse in vigilance.
keyMyths.source: Various folklore collections and theatrical performances

sources.author: Helen Craig McCullough

associations.name: Courage
associations.significance: Tsuna's unwavering bravery in the face of supernatural threats is a defining characteristic.

associations.name: Loyalty
associations.significance: His steadfast devotion to Minamoto no Yorimitsu and his duties as a samurai highlights the importance of loyalty in feudal Japan.

associations.name: Sword
associations.significance: As a samurai, Tsuna's skill with the sword is central to his identity and his ability to combat demons and other enemies.

associations.name: Oni
associations.significance: Tsuna's battles against oni and other supernatural creatures symbolize the struggle against evil and the protection of humanity.

associations.name: Modoribashi Bridge
associations.significance: The Modoribashi Bridge is a key location in the story of Tsuna severing the arm of the demon Ibaraki-doji, solidifying his reputation as a demon slayer.

associations.name: Severed Arm
associations.significance: The severed arm of Ibaraki-doji represents Tsuna's victory over the supernatural and his power to vanquish evil.

cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: Both are renowned for their incredible strength and their quests to defeat monstrous creatures.
Both are powerful warriors who protect humanity from supernatural threats, often battling demons and monsters.
Both are legendary heroes known for their exceptional martial prowess and their roles in defending their people.

worshipPractices.name: Memorials
worshipPractices.description: While not directly worshipped, Watanabe no Tsuna is often honored in local festivals and historical reenactments that celebrate the legends of Minamoto no Yorimitsu and his retainers. These events serve as a way to remember and pay respect to the samurai's bravery and contributions to Japanese folklore.

cultural.modernLegacy: Watanabe no Tsuna remains a popular figure in Japanese popular culture, appearing in numerous books, movies, anime, and video games. His stories continue to inspire and entertain, reinforcing his image as a symbol of courage, loyalty, and the triumph of good over evil. He is often depicted as a powerful and skilled warrior, embodying the ideals of the samurai class.

extendedContent.title: Early Life and Origins
extendedContent.content: Details about Watanabe no Tsuna's early life are scarce, shrouded in the mists of legend. He is believed to have been born into the Watanabe clan, a family with ties to the Minamoto clan. His early training focused on martial arts, swordsmanship, and the code of Bushido, the way of the warrior. Tsuna quickly distinguished himself as a formidable warrior, possessing exceptional strength and skill. These abilities brought him to the attention of Minamoto no Yorimitsu, also known as Raiko, a powerful and influential figure in the Imperial court. Tsuna became one of Yorimitsu's most trusted retainers, joining the elite group known as the Shitennō, the Four Heavenly Kings, who served as Yorimitsu's personal guard and were renowned for their loyalty and combat prowess. This marked the beginning of Tsuna's legendary career as a demon slayer and a hero of Japanese folklore.

extendedContent.title: Greatest Quests and Battles
extendedContent.content: Watanabe no Tsuna's most famous exploits involve battling supernatural creatures and demons, solidifying his reputation as a skilled demon slayer. One of his most celebrated tales is the encounter with Ibaraki-doji, a powerful oni who terrorized Kyoto. Disguised as a beautiful woman, Ibaraki-doji attempted to trick Tsuna at the Modoribashi Bridge, but Tsuna saw through the disguise and engaged the demon in combat. In the ensuing struggle, Tsuna severed Ibaraki-doji's arm. Another famous tale involves Tsuna accompanying Yorimitsu to Mount Oe to defeat Shuten-doji, a fearsome oni king. Disguised as monks, they infiltrated Shuten-doji's lair and, after intoxicating him with poisoned sake, launched a surprise attack. Tsuna played a crucial role in the battle, displaying his incredible strength and swordsmanship to defeat the oni and restore peace to the land. These quests showcase Tsuna's bravery, skill, and unwavering loyalty to Yorimitsu.

extendedContent.title: Legacy and Enduring Influence
extendedContent.content: Watanabe no Tsuna's legacy extends far beyond his lifetime, solidifying his place as a prominent figure in Japanese folklore and popular culture. He is remembered as a symbol of courage, loyalty, and the triumph of good over evil. His tales continue to be retold in various forms, including traditional Noh plays, Kabuki theater, and modern media such as anime, manga, and video games. The story of Tsuna severing Ibaraki-doji's arm at the Modoribashi Bridge remains one of the most iconic images associated with him. His character often embodies the ideal samurai, possessing exceptional martial skills, unwavering loyalty, and a strong sense of justice. Watanabe no Tsuna's enduring influence is a testament to the power of his stories and the values he represents, continuing to inspire and entertain audiences for generations to come.

shortDescription: A legendary samurai of the Heian period, Watanabe no Tsuna served Minamoto no Yorimitsu and was famed for his courage and demon-slaying abilities.
